inputs.prompt: Title: 'The Night Baker'. Subject: A 73-year-old baker who has worked the night shift at a family bakery for 50 years, baking bread while the city sleeps. Story arc: Routine and devotion, but also isolation — his family is urging him to retire. Duration: 15 min. Narrator: Warm, observational, not sentimental. Clips: Maria (wife, 70): 'He leaves at midnight. I wake up when he gets back at six. We've done that every day for fifty years.' (15 secs). Hans (baker, 73): 'The dough doesn't care what time it is. It rises when it's ready. I've learned patience from bread.' (12 secs). Daughter Lena (42): 'We want him to stop. But I think if he stops, he stops being himself.' (10 secs). Actuality: Kneading dough, oven door, delivery van starting, pre-dawn street sounds.

Title: 'The Bench'. Subject: A park bench memorial and the stories people tell when they sit on it. Duration: 10 min. Narrator: Reflective. Clips: Woman (60s): 'My husband proposed here. Now I come alone.' (8 secs). Teenager: 'It's just a bench. I don't know why people care so much.' (6 secs). Actuality: park sounds, birds, distant traffic.

Title: 'After the Factory'. Subject: A small town in northern France where the main employer — a textile factory — closed 5 years ago. The documentary follows three residents navigating what comes after. Duration: 20 min. Narrator: Journalistic, measured, empathetic. Clips: Pierre (former foreman, 58): 'They told us on a Friday. By Monday the gates were locked. Thirty years — gone in a weekend.' (14 secs). Amelie (mayor, 45): 'People blame me. They think I could have stopped it. I couldn't. But I have to build something from what's left.' (16 secs). Youssef (young entrepreneur, 28): 'Everyone is leaving. I stayed because someone has to prove this town isn't finished.' (11 secs). Actuality: Empty factory exterior, town square morning market, church bell.
